video_title: GameStop CEO Ryan Cohen’s $56B Plan to Take Over eBay
gist: All-In interviews Ryan Cohen on his playbook and his live activist bid for eBay. He built Chewy (sold 2017 for $3.35B) on recurring-revenue/sticky-cohort + supplier cost discipline, then turned GameStop around (collectibles now 42% of revenue, $9.7B cash, FCF positive, buyback) after admitting the 'make it like Chewy' strategy was wrong. He's now bidding 50/50 cash/stock for eBay (with $500M of his own money), arguing eBay under-earns its marketplace moat (GMV/users down, opex >50% of revenue, alienated sellers) and laying out a three-part plan: cut $2B of cost, build live commerce (~$400B TAM) using GameStop's 1,600 stores, and create a liquidity marketplace for in-game digital items. Board has rejected; 'I'm going all in.'

insights.claim: Cohen built Chewy by pivoting from jewelry to pet food specifically for the recurring/consumable revenue, sticky cohorts and a fragmented, undisrupted market — pairing Amazon-grade supply chain with neighborhood-pet-store service to reach billions in revenue on negative working capital (low capital consumption).
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: The repeatable Cohen playbook: pick recurring-revenue categories with sticky cohorts and out-execute on supply chain — the lens for reading his GameStop/eBay moves.

insights.claim: Cohen runs supplier relationships as transactional, not relational — 'if suppliers are sending us gifts, we're overpaying; if they never want to speak to us again, we're getting the right price' — treating execution as a 'game of pennies' where the gap between failure and success is pennies red vs black.
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: A hard-nosed cost-discipline ethos that he ports across companies — relevant to margin expansion at GameStop and his eBay cost-cut thesis.
insights.quote: if our suppliers are telling us they never want to speak to us again, it means we're getting the right price

insights.claim: Cohen hires for 'will overkill' (drive over resume), seeking diehards, and says 'A's only put up with A's'; he sold Chewy in 2017 for $3.35B (it later traded ~$20B+), framing his ambition as 'life is too short to do it small.'
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: Signals the scale of ambition behind the eBay bid — he deliberately pursues big, hard targets over easy organic growth.
insights.quote: life is too short to do it small

insights.claim: Cohen targeted GameStop precisely because it was the most-hated, heavily-shorted name ('running into a burning house'), with an initial thesis of surviving to the next console cycle; he filed a 13D above 5%, joined the board in 2021 with Chewy colleagues, and the company raised ~$1.7B to wipe out its debt amid the squeeze.
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: Confirms a contrarian, deep-out-of-favor entry style — buying maximum pessimism then driving change as an owner-operator.

insights.claim: Cohen admits his first GameStop strategy — making it 'more like Chewy' with e-commerce hires — was wrong; he pivoted to maniacal cost-cutting and a focus on pre-owned and collectibles (trading cards via the trade-in model).
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: A candid model-error lesson: physical retail (sticky inventory) needs a different playbook than recurring e-commerce.
insights.quote: the strategy was to make GameStop more like Chewy. And that was the wrong strategy

insights.claim: GameStop's turnaround metrics: collectibles are now 42% of revenue ($350M), Q1 revenue $835M (+14% YoY), SG&A cut from $228M to $202M, with $9.7B cash, $333M free cash flow and a newly authorized buyback.
insights.speaker: Dave (All-In)
insights.so_what: Hard evidence GameStop is an operating business, not just a meme stock — the foundation for using its shares as M&A currency.

insights.claim: Cohen is bidding to acquire and run eBay at a premium with a 50% cash / 50% stock structure, putting $500M of his own money in; the eBay board rejected the offer citing financing uncertainty and has refused to engage.
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: A live, hostile-leaning activist situation — the structure (stock-for-stock at premium) and board resistance define the catalyst path.
insights.quote: I'm putting 500 million of my own money into this transaction

insights.claim: Cohen's eBay critique: a first-mover marketplace moat that stagnated post-founder — GMV down, active users down ~30 million, operating expenses above 50% of revenue for a no-inventory business — because management alienated sellers (who ARE the customer in a marketplace) and leans on outside consultants instead of fixing root causes.
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: The value-gap thesis: eBay under-earns its franchise, fixable by re-engaging sellers — the core of the activist case.

insights.claim: Cohen's three-part eBay plan: (1) pull $2B of costs from a ~$5.5B opex base / $2.4B of marketing that drove no user growth; (2) attack live commerce (TAM ~$400B, fast-growing) using eBay's 1,600 stores as creator studios and fulfillment nodes; (3) a previously-undisclosed idea — build a marketplace providing LIQUIDITY for in-game digital items (skins, weapons), which 'actually have real utility' unlike NFTs, in a market he thinks could dwarf physical collectibles.
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: A concrete value-creation roadmap; the in-game-items liquidity marketplace is a novel, large-TAM optionality not yet priced in.
insights.quote: I would use eBay to uh to provide liquidity for in-game digital items

insights.claim: Cohen would NOT take on first-party inventory (marketplace model only) and won't go head-to-head with Amazon — but argues Amazon is over-earning on seller margins, leaving sellers hungry for an alternative they stay on only for reach.
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: Frames an opening for a seller-friendly marketplace — the strategic wedge if eBay re-engages its base.

insights.claim: On mechanics: a vote to lower eBay's special-meeting threshold from 20% to 10% narrowly failed (so 20% is still required to call one), the CEO's golden parachute exceeds $100M, and Cohen signals escalation paths (tender/hostile, shareholder-base turnover) — 'I'm going all in.'
insights.speaker: Ryan Cohen
insights.so_what: Maps the escalation toolkit and procedural hurdles — key for handicapping how the proxy contest plays out.
